coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots regulate crosslinking of functionalized three-dimensional graphene networks decorated with p-phenylenediamine for superior performance flexible solid-state supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Pengcheng
Kang, Hongxing
Dong, Yiwei
coredata.dc:description: p-phenylenediamine (PPD) are preferred as a functional monomer to modify graphene oxide for functionalized three-dimensional (3D) graphene networks by a facile hydrothermal method, and carbon dots (CDs) served as fill factor to regulate the crosslinking degree for optimized functionalized 3D graphene networks (rGO-PPDs-CDs), which is in favor fast-moving of ions to achieve higher capacitance performance. As a result, rGO-PPDs-CDs exhibits promising potential as an activate electrode material in supercapacitor, revealing optimal specific capacitance (468.7 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g) and excellent rate capability (72.9% even up to high current density of 100 A/g). Furthermore, flexible solid-state SCs (FSSCs) explored using rGO-PPD-CDs, exhibit high specific capacitance (321.9 F/g at 0.5 A/g), and maximum specific energy of 10.99 Wh/kg and highest specific power of 10.09 kW/kg. In addition, FSSCs also maintain the original performance even in a harsh environment (bending state or twisting state). The excellent electrochemical performance of FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CDs makes it an attractive candidate for energy-storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: p-Phenylene diamine
Carbon dots
Regulate crosslinking degree
Functionalized graphene networks
Flexible solid-state supercapacitors

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of functionalized three-dimensional graphene networks decorated with p-phenylenediamine and carbon dots (rGO-PPD-CDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) GO, (b) CDs, the inset in (b) shows the corresponding size distribution of CDs and (c and d) rGO-PPD-CDs, and the CDs marked with red cycle.
SEM images of (a) rGO, (b) rGO-CDs, (c and d) rGO-PPD, (e and f) rGO-PPD-CD1, (g and h) rGO-PPD-CD3 and (i and j) rGO-PPD-CD5.
(a) SEM images of rGO-PPD-CDs and (b-d) EDX element mapping of C, N and O.
(a) XPS survey spectra of rGO, rGO-CDs, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs, (b) C 1s spectra of rGO-PPD-CDs and (c) N 1s spectra of rGO-PPD-CDs.

(a) Photographs of CDs solution under 365 nm UV-light, 5 mg/mL CDs solution, 20 mg of CDs, rGO, rGO-PPD, and rGO-PPD-CDs, respectively. (b) Specific surface area of CDs, rGO, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs, respectively. (c) Pore size of CDs, rGO, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs, respectively.

(a) Comparison of CV curves of rGO, rGO-CDs, rGO-PPD, rGO-PPD-CD1, rGO-PPD-CD2, rGO-PPD-CD3, rGO-PPD-CD4 and rGO-PPD-CD5 at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. (b) Comparison of GCD curves of rGO, rGO-CDs, rGO-PPD, rGO-PPD-CD1, rGO-PPD-CD2, rGO-PPD-CD3, rGO-PPD-CD4 and rGO-PPD-CD5 at a current density of 0.5 A/g. (c) CV curves of rGO-PPD-CD3 at a scan rate from 5 mV/s to 100 mV/s. (d) Log i versus log ν plot of the anodic peak currents. (e) Capacitive ratio-controlled charge storage processes at varied scan rates. (f) GCD curves of rGO-PPD-CD3 at a current density from 0.5 A/g to 100 A/g. (g) Specific capacitance of these samples from 0.5 A/g to 100 A/g. (h) Nyquist plots of rGO, rGO-CDs, rGO-PPD, rGO-PPD-CD1, rGO-PPD-CD2, rGO-PPD-CD3, rGO-PPD-CD4 and rGO-PPD-CD5, and inserted enlarged Nyquist plots. (i) Cycling stability of rGO-PPD-CD3 scanned at 100 mV/s.
Illustration of the possible charge transition states for rGO-PPD-CDs electrodes.

(a) CV curves of FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD3 at a scan rate from 5 mV/s to 100 mV/s (b) GCD curves of FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD3 at a current density from 0.5 A/g to 50 A/g. (c) Nyquist plots of FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD3. (d) Specific capacitances of FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD3. (e) Cycling stability of FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD3 at current density of 10 A/g. (f) Ragone plots of the FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD3 vs reported SCs.

(a) Schematically showing how FSSCs are bent at under different state (flat, bent, twist and restore flat state). (b) CV curves of FSSCs while SCs are bent under different state (flat, bent, twist and restore flat state). (c) CV curves of FSSCs scanned at 100 mV/s (d) GCD curves of single FSSCs (black), two FSSCs in series (red) and three FSSCs in series (blue). (e) Photograph of a red LED powered by three FSSCs in series.
Schematic diagram of the preparation process of rGO-PPD-CDs and assembly of flexible solid-state supercapacitors (FSSCs).
Specific surface area and pore size of CDs, rGO, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs, respectively.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots regulate crosslinking of functionalized three-dimensional graphene networks decorated with p-phenylenediamine for superior performance flexible solid-state supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Institute of Polymer Science and Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Institute of Polymer Science and Engineering
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry and Institute of Polymer Science and Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: p-phenylenediamine (PPD) are preferred as a functional monomer to modify graphene oxide for functionalized three-dimensional (3D) graphene networks by a facile hydrothermal method, and carbon dots (CDs) served as fill factor to regulate the crosslinking degree for optimized functionalized 3D graphene networks (rGO-PPDs-CDs), which is in favor fast-moving of ions to achieve higher capacitance performance. As a result, rGO-PPDs-CDs exhibits promising potential as an activate electrode material in supercapacitor, revealing optimal specific capacitance (468.7 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g) and excellent rate capability (72.9% even up to high current density of 100 A/g). Furthermore, flexible solid-state SCs (FSSCs) explored using rGO-PPD-CDs, exhibit high specific capacitance (321.9 F/g at 0.5 A/g), and maximum specific energy of 10.99 Wh/kg and highest specific power of 10.09 kW/kg. In addition, FSSCs also maintain the original performance even in a harsh environment (bending state or twisting state). The excellent electrochemical performance of FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CDs makes it an attractive candidate for energy-storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Portable and wearable devices attracted people's increasing attention, which gradually come into our lives and is profoundly changing the way we live. The energy storage devices can provide an efficient energy supply and determine the overall performance of portable and wearable devices. Thus, suitable energy storage devices are developed to adapt the portable and wearable devices [1–6]. Supercapacitors (SCs), also called electrochemical capacitor, are widely recognized as an important class of energy storage devices. The SCs can shorten the enormous gap of energy density and power density between conventional capacitors and batteries, which exhibit a variety of application in many fields, such as portable and wearable devices, stand-by power systems and hybrid electric vehicles [7–14].

Many efforts have been devoted to develop promising materials to promote the property of SCs. Among them, graphene, which is a very famous unique 2D material, exhibits promising advantages for SCs electrode materials, such as high specific surface area, excellent conductivity, and remarkable mechanical property [15–18]. Thus, varies of graphene electrode materials are designed and developed for high-performance SCs [18–22]. For example, Chen's group proved that reduced graphene materials could be used in supercapacitor and exhibited high electrochemical property [19]. However, the parallel restacking of graphene sheet to form graphite-like powders can decrease the specific surface area and result in low capacitance. In order to pursuit of high capacitance, different types of graphene materials were designed and fabricated as electrode materials. 3D graphene hydrogel, hydrogel film and porous graphene aerogel were proved to be effective to prevent of restacking of graphene sheets for construction of ideal electrode materials [20, 21, 23–26]. For example, self-stacked solvated graphene films was employed to utilize in supercapacitor, which achieved the specific capacitance of 245 F/g at current density of 0.5 A/g [20]. Holey graphene constructed macrostructures materials were used as binder-free supercapacitor electrodes with excellent specific capacitance of 283 F/g at a current density of 1 A/g [27]. In addition, the introduction of foreign materials filled in middle of the graphene sheets were confirmed to be effective to limit the restacking of graphene sheets for high active surface area, such as carbon dots (CDs), ethanediamine, PEI, cellulose nanofibers (CNFs), CNTs, melamine resin, and so on were all recognized as desired fillers [28–34]. 3D graphene network decorated with CDs exhibited excellent specific capacitance of 308 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g [28]. However, mainly depending on electrical double-layer capacitor (EDLC), electrode materials based on graphene cannot achieve more superior electrochemical performance. Thus, many high theoretical capacitance materials are purposefully selected to modify graphene for ideal materials with higher capacitive properties, such as polyaniline (PANI), p-phenylene diamine (PPD), hydroquinone and 2-aminoanthraquinone, which have been proved to be effective to improve the electrochemical performance of electrode materials [35–39]. Graphene networks functionalized with PPD monomers achieved a perfect specific capacitance of 422 F/g due to the contribution of pseudo-capacitive [37]. So increasing the EDLC and pseudo-capacitance is the best choice for the fabrication of electrode materials with excellent electrochemical performance.

Herein, functionalized 3D graphene networks (rGO-PPD-CDs) was constructed by decorating with p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and employing the CDs as fill factor to regulate the crosslinking degree through a facile hydrothermal method. The well-defined rGO-PPD-CDs exhibited obvious 3D network structure, and it is in favor fast-moving of ions to achieve higher capacitance performance (468.7 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g) and perfect rate capability (72.9% even up to 100 A/g). Furthermore, rGO-PPD-CDs was utilized to assemble flexible solid-state SCs (FSSCs), which also exhibited high specific capacitance, good cycling stability, and excellent mechanical property.

Graphite powder (325 mesh, Qingdao Huatai Lubricant Sealing S&T Co. Ltd.) was directly used after obtained. p-Phenylenediamine (PPD) (Aladdin Reagent Inc.) was received without further purification. Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA, Mw=89 000-98 000 g/mol, 99+% hydrolyzed) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), citric acid anhydrous (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were analytical grade. GO was prepared with modified Hummer's method [40].

CDs was synthesized via one-step microwave pyrolysis [41]. Typically, 2.0 g of CA and 1.04 mL of EDA was dissolved in 20 mL DI-water with a 100 mL beaker. The beaker with this mixture solution was subsequently heated for about 4 min in a domestic microwave oven (700 W). After dialysis with dialysis tube (MWCO of 1000) for 2 days against DI-water, CDs was obtained after freeze-dried for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of functionalized three-dimensional graphene networks decorated with p-phenylenediamine and carbon dots (rGO-PPD-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: rGO-PPD-CDs were synthesized using GO solution, PPD and CDs with hydrothermal method. 70 mL of GO suspension (1 mg/mL) mixed with 70 mg PPD and CDs (5 mg/mL) was ultrasonic for 30 min and then transferred to Teflon autoclave liner (100 mL). The content of CDs was fixed at 0 to 5 wt% of the total amount of GO and PPD. The reaction was heated to 90 ºC and kept for 1 h in oven, then the temperature was raised to 180 ºC for another 12 h. After down to room temperature, the sample of rGO-PPD-CDs was centrifuged and washed completely with DI-water. The final product was obtained after freeze-dried for 48 h.
The comparison sample of rGO-CDs was prepared by a similar step only without PPD. rGO was prepared with the same fabrication process.

The free-standing electrodes were fabricated as the following steps: active electrode material slurry, which was mixed with active material (rHGO-PPD-CDs), PVDF, and acetylene black (AC) with a weight ratio of 8:1:1 in NMP, was dropped onto a flexible carbon film. After dried at 80 ºC for 6 h, the flexible electrodes were coated with PVA-H2SO4 gel and then stored to evaporate the excess water in air. At last, FSSCs were fabricated with two as-prepared electrodes pressed together with a separator (NKK cellulose) caught in the middle.

The morphologies of synthesized samples were evaluated by a field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200c). The composite of these samples were analyzed with PHI-5702 multi-functional X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The Raman spectra were investigated using the Thermo Scientific DXR Raman Microscope. The corresponding hydrodynamic size distribution of CDs was tested by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) technology (BI-200SM equipment, Brookhaven Instruments Corporation, USA).

The electrochemical performance of electrodes were performed in a standard three-electrode system using 1 M H2SO4 as electrolyte by CHI 660E workstation, with platinum foil as counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode. Furthermore, the properties of FSSCs were investigated based on cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) tests and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using two-electrode system.

EDS mapping analysis was characterized and the results were presented in Fig. 3 . These elements of C, N and O were uniformly distributed throughout the sample of rGO-PPD-CDs, indicating successful introduction and uniform distribution of PPD and CDs (Fig. 3b-d). Furthermore, XPS were carried out to evaluate compositions of the samples in the preparation process. The survey XPS spectra of rGO, rGO-CDs, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs are shown in Fig. 4 a. It is noted that three peaks of C1s, O1s and N1s exist in all samples. After introduction of CDs, the content of N element increases to 3.83% (rGO-CDs) compared to rGO (1.73%). The introduction of PPD also can improve the N element of rGO-PPD-CDs, and the value rises to 6.15 %. As a result, the rGO-PPD-CDs reveals highest N element content, and the value improves to 7.28% because of the contribution of PPD and CDs. In addition, the C1s spectra of rGO-PPD-CDs was measured and the data was presented in Fig. 4b, the high-resolution C1s spectrum of rGO-PPD-CDs was proposed to five peaks at 282.9, 285.2, 284.2, 286.5 and 288.5 eV, which were assigned to the groups of C=C, C-C, C-N, C-O-C, and C=O, respectively. Fig. 4c presented the N1s spectrum of rGO-PPD-CDs, which was fitted into three peaks locating at 398.9 (-N=), 397.8 (-NH-) and 400.1(-N+-), respectively.[37, 42, 43] Furthermore, the content of PPD embedded in different samples is measured by testing supernatant of the reactants at 239 nm (maximum absorption wavelength) with UV-vis technology (Fig. S1a) and the standard curve (Fig. S1b), the results are presented in Table S1. As a result, almost of the PPD molecules are embedded in graphene sheet, and the content of PPD embedded in rGO-PPD-CD3 is 0.974 mg/mg (PPD/rGO).

Raman spectra were used to investigate the fabrication process of rGO-PPD-CDs. All samples reveal obvious G and D bands shown in Fig. S1. As a result, the ratio of D/G increases to 1.199 (rGO-CDs) from 1.106 (rGO), because the abundant surface active groups of CDs cannot be reduced in large quantity. In addition, the introduction of PPD leads to larger ratio of D/G (1.363) due to the reaction between GO and PPD, which can cause different from reduced GO and results in higher degree of disorder. As last, CDs and PPD are used to modify the rGO, and the ratio of D/G decrease to 1.308 because the PPD can react with CDs and GO at the same time. Obviously, the wavenumber of G band shifts to 1583.4 cm−1 (rGO-PPD-CDs and rGO-PPD) from 1598.9 cm−1 (rGO) because the reaction of PPD and GO affects the further reduction of graphene [28, 36, 38].

CDs can be dispersed stable in aqueous, and emit a strong blue fluorescence shown in Fig. 5 . In order to compare with the apparent volume of rGO, rGO-CDs, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs, the photograph of these samples (25 mg) is presented in Fig. 5a. The existence of CDs can absorb in graphene sheets based on electrostatic force and prevent the agglomeration of graphene sheets, and the volume of rGO-CDs is obviously larger than that of rGO. In addition, the introduction of PPD can crosslink the GO sheets and the existence of CDs can enhance the distance of rGO sheets for better 3D structure. As a result, rGO-PPD-CDs exhibits a fluffy state. Obviously, the apparent volume of rGO-PPD-CDs is several times than that of rGO. Furthermore, BET measurement was performed to evaluate the specific surface area of CDs, rGO, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs (Fig. 5b and c) and the results were presented in Table 1 . The specific surface area of rGO is about 225 m2/g and the average size is 6.08 nm, which is mainly resulted by the accumulation of graphene sheets. However, the fluffy rGO-PPD possesses smaller specific surface areas, the value is only 98 m2/g and the average size is 9.40 nm, because the PPD molecules embedded in rGO-PPD cannot adsorb N2 molecule, which results in small specific surface area and wide aperture distribution. The original CDs only exhibits 2.43 m2/g specific surface areas due to serious reunions. However, CDs can uniform embedded in rGO-PPD-CDs and contribute part of the surface area to adsorb N2 molecule. As a result, the specific surface area improves to 130 m2/g. Otherwise, the average size enlarges to 9.40 nm due to the embedding of PPD molecules crosslinks the graphene and CDs and blocks the aggregation of graphene sheets so as to results in larger pore size.

The electrochemical properties of rGO, rGO-CDs, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs were investigated using 3-electrode system configuration with 1 M H2SO4 as electrolyte. Fig. 6 a presented the CV curves of rGO, rGO-CDs, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs electrodes at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. It is noted that the introduction of CDs can improve the capacitive performance based on EDLCs. Obviously, the CV curves of these electrode materials with PPD exhibit distinct faradaic redox peaks locating to reversible redox transitions of aromatic amine, which is similar with the polyaniline structure formed based on PPD crosslinked GO [37, 38], and the possible charge transition states of rGO-PPD-CDs is shown in Fig. 7 . The rGO-PPD-CD3 exhibits the largest integral area and reveals the most excellent capacitance performance due to combined effect of PPD and CDs based on pseudo-capacitance and EDLCs. As shown in Fig. 6b, GCD curves of all samples were tested at a current density of 0.5 A/g, and GCD curves showed the same trend as the results of CV curves. Indeed, GCD curves of rGO-PPD-CDs agree well with the CV curves, the obvious plateau is caused by reversible redox transitions of aromatic amine. Obviously, the rGO-PPD-CD3 electrode possesses largest discharge time indicating the most excellent capacitance performance among of these samples. Furthermore, the complete CV tests of all samples at a scan rate from 5 mV/s to 100 mV/s were measured and the results were presented in Fig. 6c and Fig. S2. The charge storage kinetics of rGO-PPD-CD3 was investigated based on CV curves (Fig.6c), and the capacitive effects can be calculated by the equation:
(1)
i = a
ν b
where a and b are variables, and the b value is obtained from the slope of log (i)-log (ν) plot. When the b value is close to 1, the electrochemical process is mainly based on capacitance dominated [44, 45]. As shown in Fig. 6d, the b value is 0.94, very close to 1, revealing the charge transfer process of capacitance classification. To further calculate the capacitive contribution, the results are analyzed according to the following equations (2).
(2)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
(3)
i /
v
1 / 2
=
k 1
/
v
1 / 2
+
k 2
Where the k1 and k2 are constants. The capacitive contribution of rGO-PPD-CD3 is presented in Fig. 6e, the proportion of the capacitive effect gradually rise from 64.34% to 93.27 % when the scan rate increases from 5 to 100 mV/s. The results reveal that capacitive contribution plays a major role in the charge storage process. Moreover, the CV curves reveals the similar capacitance characteristic as the GCD curves at different current densities from 0.5 A/g to 100 A/g (Fig. 6d and Fig. S2). The specific capacitance (Cs ) of these electrode materials were calculated based on the equation (S1) and the results were presented in Fig. 6e. Obviously, the rGO shows the worst specific capacitance, and the value only reaches to 177.1 F/g at 0.5 A/g. The introduction of CDs can improve the capacitance and the Cs improves to 252.9 F/g. The PPD can demonstrate the pseudo-capacitance for electrode materials, and the Cs obviously increases to 311.8 F/g. The CDs and PPD can promote the capacitive performance based on EDLCs and pseudo-capacitive property, thus the rGO-PPD-CDs electrodes exhibit more excellent electrochemical performance. It is noted that the rGO-PPD-CD3 with the content of CDs of 3% reveals the best electrochemical performance up to 468.7 F/g at 0.5 A/g. Furthermore, the Cs can maintain 341.6 F/g even up to 100 A/g meaning excellent rate capacitance of 72.9% because of the porous structure constructed by CDs and PPD supplies the rapid ion transfer rate. However, the excessive content of CDs can decrease the demonstration of pseudo-capacitive property and result in low specific capacitance, and the rGO-PPD-CD4 and rGO-PPD-CD5 exhibit lower Cs than rGO-PPD-CD3. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed to understand the electrochemical behavior of the doped graphene composite. As shown in Fig. 6f, the Nyquist plots are used to analyze the charge transfer kinetics and resistive behavior of rGO, rGO-CDs, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs. The rGO and rGO-CDs exhibits smaller resistance (Rs ), which is 0.904 Ω and 0.757 Ω, respectively. That indicates high conductivity and low contact resistance between electrodes and current collectors because of the better reduction of GO. However, the PPD molecules can catch in the middle of GO and decreases the conductivity of PPD modified graphene composite. Thus, the Rs reduces to the vicinity of 1.15 Ω. Furthermore, rGO-PPD and rGO-PPD-CDs reveal smaller charge transfer resistance (Rct ) meaning the total resistance at the electrolyte/electrode interface. In addition, Warburg resistance (ZW) is indicated as the slope of the Nyquist plots, which depend on ion diffusion/transport in the electrolyte to the surface of electrode. rGO-PPD-CDs exhibits higher slope than rGO-PPD indicating that the CDs can promote the ion movement due to excellent porous morphology.

As active electrode material of supercapacitor, cycle stability is a very important parameter. As shown in Fig. 6g, the cycle stability of rGO-PPD-CD3 was performed by CV tests at a scan rate of 100 mV/s, the capacitance retention maintained about 74.9% after 5000 cycles and the value kept about 69% after 10000 cycles. In the preparation process, partial PPD molecular can adsorb on the surface of graphene sheet based on aromatic stacking interaction, which is unstable and easily separate from graphene sheet in the charge-discharge process and result in decline of cycle stability.

The flexible solid-state supercapacitors (FSSCs) were assembled using free-standing electrode based on rGO-PPD-CD3. The detailed fabrication steps are particular described in the Experimental section. Furthermore, the electrochemical performance of FSSCs was analyzed through CV, GCD and EIS curves (Fig. 8 ). CV curves, scanned from 5 mV/s to 100 mV/s and measured in the potential range from 0 to 1.0 V (Fig. 8a), exhibit obvious redox peaks indicating pseudo-capacitive, which agree well with GCD curves (Fig. 8b). GCD curves were performed at a range from 0.5 A/g to 50 A/g. Obviously, GCD curves show approximately triangular shape without significant voltage drop, which implies small internal resistance of 1.998 Ω (Fig. 8c). The Cs of FSSCs was obtained according to the equation based on the GCD curves and the data was presented in Fig. 8d. The maximum Cs is 322 F/g at 0.5 A/g, and the Cs can maintain 238.1 F/g even up to 50 A/g. As a result, the FSSCs exhibits excellent rate capacity, which can retain about 74% of original specific capacitance even up to 50 A/g. Furthermore, the FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD3 show quite good cycle stability and the Cs can keep about 76 % of initial capacitance value after 5000 cycles investigating by GCD tests at a current density of 10 A/g (Fig. 8e). As shown in Fig. 8f, the Ragone plot (specific energy vs. specific power) of FSSCs possesses maximum specific energy of 10.99 Wh/kg at a specific power of 0.248 kW/kg, and the specific power reaches to 10.09 kW/kg at specific energy of 2.246 Wh/kg. The FSSCs exhibits excellent performance and the value is comparable or higher than some reported FSSCs systems, such as FSSC based on 3D-graphene/graphite paper (specific energy of 8.8 Wh/kg at specific power of 0.179 kW/kg) [46], SCs based on 3D interconnected graphene nanocapsules (specific energy of 7.97 Wh/kg at specific power of 0.04 kW/kg) [47], symmetric SCs based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene-like carbon (specific energy of 6.4 Wh/kg at specific power of 0.275 kW/ kg) [48], symmetric SCs based on porous active carbon layer modified graphene (specific energy of 7.49 Wh/kg at specific power of 0.2 kW/kg) [49], SCs based on MnO2 and graphene nanoplatelets on graphite paper (specific energy of 9.2 Wh/kg at specific power of 0.02 kW/kg) [50], FSSCs based on CDs reinforced PPy/graphene nanoplatelets on flexible eggshell membranes (specific energy of about 5.9 Wh/kg at specific power of 0.125 kW/kg) [51], FSSC constructed based on rHGO-PPD (specific energy of about 11.71 Wh/kg at specific power of 0.249 kW/kg) [38].

The mechanical property is a very important parameter for flexible energy storage devices. For testing the mechanical property of FSSCs, the FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD3 were placed in different contortions state, such as flat, bending, and twisting state (Fig. 9
a), and CV testes at a scan rate of 100 mV/s were used to evaluate the capacitance performance (Fig. 9b). It is noted that the FSSCs show approximately overlapped CV curves even in bending state or twisting state, and the CV curves overlap when the FSSCs returns to its original state.

FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD3 were explored the potential applications, tandem SCs constructed by two or three FSSCs devices are assembled and the CV curves at 100 mV/s and GCD curves at a current density of 1 A/g are shown in Fig. 9c and d. The potential window is 1.0 V for a single FSSC and 2.0 V for the tandem FSSCs with two FSSCs, the potential can extend to 3.0 V for the tandem FSSCs with three FSSCs. It is clearly that the charge-discharge curve of the tandem SCs constructed with two FSSCs and three FSSCs almost overlap with single FSSCs, which indicating the same performance of each FSSCs in the tandem FSSCs. Furthermore, a red LED can be lighted up by three FSSCs in series (Fig. 9e). These results signify that FSSCs based on rGO-PPD-CD have potential applications as flexible energy-storage devices.

In summary, functionalized 3D graphene networks decorated with PPD and CDs (rGO-PPD-CDs) were successfully fabricated by a facile hydrothermal method. The introduction of CDs could adjust the crosslinking degree of rGO-PPD. When the CD was raised to 3%, the well-defined rGO-PPD-CDs exhibits obvious 3D network structure and highly capacitive property as an activate electrode, which possessed optimal specific capacitance (468.7 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g) and excellent rate capability (72.9% even up to high current density of 100 A/g). Furthermore, FSSCs was explored using rGO-PPD-CD3, which exhibited high specific capacitance (321.9 F/g at 0.5 A/g), good cycling stability and also displayed maximum specific energy of 10.99 Wh/kg and highest specific power of 10.09 kW/kg. In addition, FSSCs also maintained original performance even in a harsh environment (bending state or twisting state). Hence, rGO-PPD-CDs possesses great potential as an attractive candidate for supercapacitor electrode material.
Pengcheng Du: Design the experiment, calculate the results and write the manuscript. Hongxing Kang: Fabrication and investigation of the electrodes. Yiwei Dong: Test the electrochemical performance.
Pengcheng Du: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Hongxing Kang: Supervision. Yiwei Dong: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support from the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant no. lzujbky-2019-62).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.est.2020.101586.
